text: To: Members of the Executive Board
From: The Secretary
Subject: Grenada—First Review Under the Extended Credit Facility, Request for Modification of Performance Criteria, and Financing Assurances Review

Attached for consideration by the Executive Directors is a paper on the first review under the Extended Credit Facility for Grenada and its request for modification of performance criteria, and the financing assurances review, which is tentatively scheduled for discussion on Friday, November 19, 2010. A draft decision appears on pages 19–21. Unless an objection from the authorities of Grenada is received prior to the conclusion of the Board's consideration, the document will be published. Any requests for modifications for publication are expected to be received two days before the Board concludes its consideration.
Questions may be referred to Ms. Thacker (ext. 37464), Ms. Magnusson Bernard (ext. 37398), and Ms. Guscina (ext. 34836) in WHD.

Unless the Documents Section (ext. 36760) is otherwise notified, the document will be transmitted, in accordance with the procedures approved by the Executive Board and with the appropriate deletions, to the WTO Secretariat on Tuesday, November 16, 2010; and to the Caribbean Development Bank and the European Commission, following its consideration by the Executive Board.

This document, together with a supplement providing an informational annex, will shortly be posted on the extranet, a secure website for Executive Directors and member country authorities. The supplement, which is not being distributed in hard copy, will also be available in the Institutional Repository; a link can be found in the daily list (http://wwwint.imf.org/depts/sec/services/eb/dailydocumentsfull.htm) for the issuance date shown above.

INTERNATIONAL MONETARY FUND
GRENADA
First Review Under the Extended Credit Facility, Request for Modification of Performance Criteria, and Financing Assurances Review
Prepared by Western Hemisphere Department (In consultation with other departments)
Approved by Rodrigo Valdés and Jan Kees Martijn
November 5, 2010
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Context: The recovery from last year’s significant output contraction is yet to take hold, with no growth expected for 2010. Inflation has temporarily increased with the introduction of the Value Added Tax (VAT) while the external current account deficit has declined but remains high. Credit growth is muted, and NPL rates have increased moderately. Despite this difficult environment, there has been significant progress in fiscal consolidation. The effects of the collapse of the Trinidad and Tobago-based CL Financial Group have yet to be resolved and remain high on the agenda.

Arrangement: The current Extended Credit Facility (ECF) arrangement was approved by the Executive Board on April 2, 2010 with access equivalent to 75 percent of Grenada’s quota (SDR 8.775 million). The program is a successor of a 3-year ECF for 140 percent of quota. The completion of the first review, based on end-June performance criteria, would release a disbursement of SDR 1.25 million.
Program performance: All quantitative targets for end-June were met. The authorities also met all the structural benchmarks agreed under the program.

Discussions: The authorities were in broad agreement with staff’s assessment and policy recommendations. Staff recommended continuous focus on fiscal consolidation to put the debt trajectory on a sustainable path, a continuing regional strategy to deal with the fallout from the CL Financial Group collapse, and further efforts in the structural reform area to improve the business climate. The authorities and staff also agreed that should the authorities wish to move ahead with the recent application to borrow from the Export-Import Bank of China, its strategy would need to be consistent with its debt sustainability objectives and commitments under the ECF program.

Mission: A mission visited St. George’s during September 20–28, 2010 and met with Minister of Finance Burke, Permanent Secretary Antoine, other senior officials, members of the opposition, labor unions, and financial and private sector representatives. The mission comprised Ms. Thacker (Head), Ms. Guscina, Magnusson Bernard (all WHD), and Ms. Kaltani (SPR). ECCB and CDB staff also participated and Mr. Medina-Navarro (EU) attended the concluding meeting. Mr. McGoldrick (OED) joined the last two days of meetings.

I.   INTRODUCTION

1. Grenada has been hard hit by the global crisis. Following positive but modest growth in 2007 and 2008, the economy shrank by 7.7 percent in 2009. The effects of the global slowdown were concentrated in the two most important sectors of the economy, tourism and FDI-financed construction, which are dependent on developments in advanced countries. In addition, the collapse of the Trinidad and Tobago-based CL Financial Group has heightened financial sector uncertainty.

2. To mitigate the impact of the crisis, an ECF arrangement was approved by the Executive Board on April 2, 2010, with access equivalent to 75 percent of Grenada’s quota (US\$ 13.3 million). The program is a successor to a 3-year ECF for 140 percent of quota, which expired in April.1
II.   RECENT DEVELOPMENTS AND OUTLOOK

3. The recovery from last year’s significant output contraction is yet to take place. While the manufacturing sector performed well during the first half of 2010, and there are some promising signs in the agriculture sector notwithstanding the drought in the first half of the year, the two key sectors—tourism and construction—continue to be hard hit by the lack of a firm recovery in the U.S. and Europe. Tourist arrivals continued to decline, falling by 9.4 percent through the first half of the year compared with the same period in 2009, while construction activity declined by 15.9 percent during the same period, although the pace of decline has slowed.

4. While domestic demand and labor markets remain weak, efforts are being made to cushion the blow to employment. Retail sales (in real terms) fell 13.2 percent during the first half of 2010 compared to the same period in 2009, but are thought to have bottomed out. Labor demand remains subdued, with the number of new registrants into the compulsory National Insurance Scheme (NIS) down 16 percent for the first six months of 2010 compared to the corresponding period in 2009. Private sector employers are trying to use the options for shortening hours and cutting nominal pay allowed under their collective agreements to avoid outright firing of workers. Despite these measures, unemployment is thought to have increased to around 35 percent, a ten percentage point hike compared to 2008.

5. The external current account deficit narrowed to 27 percent of GDP, largely reflecting a decline in FDI-related imports. With most FDI-related construction activity on hold, lower construction-related imports outweighed the drop in tourism receipts and income.

As a result, the current account deficit narrowed in 2009. In addition, the weak domestic demand has weighed on imports. Private sector inflows (FDI and other investments) are financing the deficit. Nominal and real effective exchange rates were at their pre-crisis levels in September 2010.

6. Inflation increased to 6.3 percent (y/y) in September, reflecting a combination of factors. The one-off price effects from the introduction of VAT on February 1, 2010 combined with the pass-through from higher international fuel and food prices compared to last year contributed to the pickup in prices. Retail fuel prices continue to be adjusted in line with international price changes, with a four-week lag.

7. Private sector credit has remained flat throughout the year, while non-performing loans (NPLs) might not yet have peaked. Private sector credit grew by 4.4 percent $\left(\mathrm{y/y}\right)$ at end-August, compared to 4.5 percent $\operatorname{(y/y)}$ at end-2009. While deposits grew by 3.5 percent $\left(\mathrm{y/y}\right)$ in August, this largely reflects savings by institutional investors due to new regulations on statutory funds for insurance companies. Broad money growth still remains very low at 0.3 percent $\mathrm{(y/y)}$ at end-August. The ratio of NPLs to total loans remained at 5.9 percent at end-June, the same as at end-December 2009, although this includes significant restructuring of loans by commercial banks. While headline capital adequacy ratios remain well above prudential guidelines, the decline in provisioning is a concern.

8. Significant progress was made on fiscal consolidation through the first half of the year. Revenue collection for the first half of 2010 somewhat exceeded expectations due to better-than-projected performance of income and property taxes. Grant disbursements were faster than expected. On the expenditure side, planned savings on goods and services did not materialize (partly reflecting higher-than-expected fuel prices) but this was offset by containing transfers and subsidies and limiting capital outlays. As a result, the primary deficit (excluding grants) was smaller than the adjusted target for end-June under the program. The fiscal stance is aligned for a 4 percent of GDP improvement in the overall deficit in 2010 in comparison to 2009. In order to limit the negative impact of fiscal consolidation on growth, the authorities announced some temporary tax exemptions in September (see below).2

9. The authorities have applied for a loan of US\$115 million (17.8 percent of GDP) from Export-Import Bank of China (EXIM-China), to prevent losing the option of obtaining this loan. The terms of the loan, as stated by the authorities, appear to suggest a grant element of about 40-45 percent. The authorities’ aim is to eventually use the loan as a catalyst to attract private sector participation to build a luxury hotel to promote Grenada as a high-end tourist destination. The authorities have also recently signed a non-concessional loan of about US\$18.5 million (2.9 percent of GDP) with Kuwait and OPEC to complete the second phase of an agriculture feeder road project.

10. The collapse of the Trinidad and Tobago-based CL Financial Group will have fiscal costs, but they are expected to be limited. On BAICO, which was declared insolvent last year, the ECCU governments continue to work on a regional solution. The transfer of the property portfolio for BAICO is complete. The establishment of a Medical Insurance Fund to cover outstanding BAICO health insurance claims by policyholders in Grenada is expected to be launched in early November. The new company to take over BAICO’s ECCU operations has not yet been established, due to lack of identifiable resources for its capitalization.3 There has been little progress on a resolution strategy for CLICO; ECCU governments are awaiting a decision by the Barbados government, where the parent company is located.

11. The recovery is expected to remain weak in the near-term, with risks tilted to the downside. While the pace of decline in the tourism and construction sectors is expected to moderate compared to 2009, economic activity is expected to remain flat this year.4 Credit growth is expected to remain slow in the coming months as most banks have tightened lending standards. Moreover, there are significant downside risks to the recovery stemming from the still uncertain outlook in the developed countries, the major source of tourism and FDI for Grenada. A weaker recovery and continued high unemployment could also threaten fiscal consolidation by increasing spending pressures on the government. Fiscal costs for the resolution of BAICO/CLICO remain another source of risk, although ECCU governments are committed to limit these given their tight fiscal positions and the already high debt levels in many cases. Finally, the large fiscal and financial sector challenges in other ECCU countries could pose spillover risks.
III.   PERFORMANCE UNDER THE PROGRAM

12. All program-related quantitative performance criteria for end-June were met. Close monitoring and quarterly budget reviews instituted under the program were useful in helping to achieve the targets:
The target for the primary balance excluding grants (measured from below the line) was met by a margin of $\operatorname{ECS7.4}$ million (0.4 percent of GDP);
Upcoming Revisions to GDP and CPI

Rebased GDP and CPI series are expected to be launched in the coming weeks, and are expected to show a significant increase in economic activity over the last decade. The authorities have been working with CARTAC (Caribbean Regional Technical Assistance Centre) to rebase the constant price GDP to base year 2006. The main preliminary result is that the level of Grenada’s current price GDP will increase annually in the 20–25 percent range over the period 2000–2007. Real economic growth over the same period will be revised upwards from 13 to 26 percent, since deflators have also changed due to improvements in the volume estimate methodologies.
Four economic activities account for the bulk of the upward shift in GDP.

New and transparent compilation methods together with the new base year resulted in significant increases in the nominal level and real growth for the construction, wholesale and retail trade, hotels and restaurants, real estate and housing sectors. For some activities such as manufacturing, transportation and other services, there were no methodological changes apart from the rebasing, although the authorities hope to improve the data collection for these activities in the future. St. George’s University, accounting for over 10 percent of GVA, was reclassified from other services to education.

There will still be a considerable underestimation of total GDP after the revision, due to insufficient coverage of the service sector. After the reclassification of St. George’s University, other services account for only 2 percent of GDP in 2007. Given that this activity should reflect most household and business services, the present estimate is widely regarded as too low and additional efforts to extend its coverage, including the use of VAT source data, remain a top priority. Moreover, since there are at present no firm plans to extend the calculations prior to 2000, there will be a break between 1999 and 2000 after the launch of the new series.

The revised CPI series will meet international standards. A new CPI basket, including several additional items, has been developed using data from the 2007/08 Household Income and Expenditure Survey (HIES), with a strong recommendation to conduct the HIES on a five-year cycle to ensure more timely future updates of the basket than the current ten-year period. Routines for processing collected price data have also been upgraded. The new series will be re-referenced to January 2010 and historical CPI series back to 2001 have been re-calculated based on the new structure.

Domestic arrears older than 60 days were reduced by more than EC\$ 10 million during the first half of this year, meeting the end-June target by a margin of $\operatorname{ECS}0.7$ million (0.04 percent of GDP). However, the indicative target for September was missed by $\operatorname{ECS}$ 6 million, reflecting cash flow problems, but the authorities remain committed to reducing the arrears to meet the end-December program target;
No new external loans were contracted and no external arrears were accumulated as of end-June.

text: 13. The structural benchmarks under the program were also met. As agreed under the program,

Two quarterly budget reviews were conducted, at end-April and end-July, followed by the issuance of finance circulars to keep expenditures under control.   
 The VAT unit was integrated as a new division in the Inland Revenue Department, and officers in the new division are carrying out VAT audits.   
 An inventory of all the various government bank accounts has been completed and significant progress has been made in streamlining the number of accounts. By April of next year a Single Treasury Account System is expected to be established.   
 With respect to the insurance sector, the Grenada Authority for the Regulation of Financial Institutions (GARFIN) has completed the on-site inspection of two insurance companies and worked closely with the insurance sector to set up statutory insurance funds. Most insurance companies have pledged the required assets and placed them in trust with GARFIN, but the administrative procedures of placing them in a trusteeship at banks are taking longer than expected. The process is expected to be completed by end-January 2011.

text: IV.   POLICY DISCUSSIONS
Policy discussions focused on policies to ensure fiscal and debt sustainability, financial system vulnerabilities, and the need to generate growth and employment.
A.   Fiscal Policies and Debt Sustainability
Fiscal Stance for 2010

Further fiscal consolidation is planned for the rest of the year in line with the program to cement a clear downward path for the debt-to-GDP ratio. Revenues are expected to be lower in the second half of the year than in the first, reflecting lower customs revenues due in part to lower imports and a small impact from the recently announced tax exemptions. On the expenditure side, spending on goods and services has been revised up reflecting developments in the first half of the year, but these will be partially offset by lower spending on transfers while protecting the spending for vulnerable groups. Grants are expected to be higher than previously projected, reflecting the approval of the V-Flex facility by the European Union.5 Notwithstanding these developments, the primary deficit (excluding grants) is expected to decline from 7.9 percent of GDP in 2009 to 3.8 percent of GDP in 2010, broadly the same as envisaged in the program. Despite the temporary VAT exemptions for the tourism and construction sectors to stimulate the economy, the estimated fiscal impulse excluding interest payments is significantly negative for 2010, as opposed to the expansionary stance in 2009.
Fiscal Stance for 2011 and Medium-Term Fiscal Consolidation

14. Authorities remain committed to further fiscal consolidation to ensure a declining debt trajectory. The authorities are planning to adopt a three-year rolling budget framework, starting with the 2011 budget, to allow a more stable path for fiscal management. While the details of the multi-year budgets are being worked out, authorities agreed with staff that primary surpluses in the range of $2-2\%$ percent will be required over the medium term to ensure that the public debt-to-GDP ratio declines to the ECCB recommended target of 60 percent by 2020.6 However, given the difficult economic circumstances and the need to generate growth and employment, the adjustment will be phased in, as was agreed in the program. For 2011, the aim is to reduce the primary deficit (excluding grants) to 1.6 percent of GDP and to start generating surpluses from 2012 as the economy picks up. As a result, the fiscal impulse is expected to remain negative in the near-term. However, the authorities emphasized that generating growth also remains a key policy objective as this would allow a faster decline in the debt ratios and they are seeking donor assistance in this area.
15. The fiscal adjustment will rely on a multi-pronged strategy. On the revenue front, the objective is to improve the efficiency and compliance of tax collections7:

Limit tax exemptions. The authorities argued that the recently announced tax exemptions, which were undertaken without consultation with the Fund, were necessary to prevent lay-offs and sustain domestic demand given the drag in external demand. However, they reiterated that these are only temporary as announced and no new exemptions were envisaged to ensure that program objectives are not undermined (see MEFP, paragraph 10). Accordingly, they plan to pass an amended VAT law to establish a legal basis for the announced exemptions, including the expiration dates for the temporary tax exemptions (proposed structural benchmark for the second review).
Improve VAT compliance. Following the successful introduction of the VAT, the government will focus on improving compliance, including through taxpayer
education and service programs to facilitate voluntary compliance with filing and payment requirements, and expediting the ongoing establishment of a single unique Tax Identification Number.

Improve customs collection. The new Customs Act is expected to be passed before end-year. The pilot project for the upgraded customs technology platform (ASYCUDA World) and the introduction of risk-based inspections is expected to be completed by the first quarter of 2011. All ports are expected to be upgraded over the next two years. This will help to improve both the yield and efficiency of customs collection.8

text: 16. On the expenditure side, the adoption of an Action Plan to improve the overall management and efficiency of spending combined with the following measures will help to generate savings (see MEFP paragraph 13):

Improving public financial management. The authorities are implementing an Action Plan based on the recently completed Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA) assessment, with TA from CARTAC and other donors. It includes (i) a formal comprehensive commitment control system to ensure that spending units do not undertake commitments without authorization (this is currently being done through quarterly budget reviews followed by finance circulars to ministries to limit commitments as agreed under the program), (ii) steps to improve the cash management model, and (iii) a timeline for moving to a Single Treasury Account System (proposed structural benchmark for the second review). These reforms are expected to be completed over the next year and should help improve expenditure management and efficiency.

Limiting growth in the wage bill. The authorities have been negotiating with trade unions to limit wage increases given the competing needs on the budget. In addition, they plan to contain the wage bill (Grenada’s wage bill, both in terms of revenues and in terms of GDP, is among the highest in the region) by limiting the number of new hires, especially temporary staff which last year accounted for more than one percent of GDP in wage expenses, and to leave positions unfilled. In addition, the civil service review, with the assistance of the World Bank, could help to identify further savings.

Limiting capital spending. For 2011, given the financing constraints and the need to protect spending for vulnerable groups, capital spending will remain focused on priority projects. The authorities agreed that shovel-ready projects that have secured financing available should take priority over new initiatives. This would help to avoid running up arrears while ensuring that essential projects, especially those that have the highest employment generation potential, are implemented; and

Improve efficiency of spending. To reduce spending on goods and services, the authorities have set up a Waste Reduction Unit which should start functioning effectively by the end of the year. This unit is tasked to identify ways in which to reduce spending, especially on travel and communications. In addition, the Public Procurement Act is planned for passage by parliament by end-year and the Public Procurement Unit will be established by March 2011 (structural benchmark for the second review). This unit will try to generate savings through bulk procurement, including sole source contracts (for example, using one supplier across all ministries rather than each ministry acquiring what it needs). In addition, the Debt Management Unit is developing a debt management strategy (structural benchmark for the second review) that will help to identify cheaper sources of financing so as to reduce the cost of borrowing (see MEFP, paragraph 14).

17. The program provides some flexibility given the continued fragile economic environment, including the uncertainty of a recovery in external demand and high domestic unemployment. As before, program adjustors will accommodate all additional grants that may become available as well as concessional financing of up to an additional 2 percent of GDP on an annual basis. In addition, program adjustors would accommodate higher-than-programmed disbursements on existing donor-financed projects. The authorities have requested that the end-December performance criterion on non-concessional external debt be adjusted to accommodate the loans from Kuwait and OPEC as completion of the feeder road project is essential to yield the planned benefits to the agricultural sector. Staff supports this request only as an exception to the normal zero ceiling on non-concessional borrowing in view of the project’s significance in enhancing the potential of the agricultural sector and the fact that discontinuation of the project midway would undermine all potential benefits that the project was designed to have.

18. The authorities plan to request debt relief from the Paris Club (2.4 percent of total debt).9 While Fund and other donor financing is expected to close the financing gap in 2010 and the first half of 2011, additional financial support is projected to be needed during the rest of the program period. Staff’s current estimates suggest a financing gap averaging approximately $\mathrm{US}\S6$ million in 2011–13.10 Staff agreed to assist the authorities to approach the Paris Club for additional debt treatment in order to help close the medium-term financing gap (see MEFP, paragraph 18), especially as this would help them to seek comparable terms from other external creditors covering an additional 8.6 percent of GDP. The authorities also continue to pursue good faith efforts to reach a collaborative agreement with Grenada’s external commercial creditors that did not participate in the 2005 debt exchange, on terms comparable to the original exchange.11

19. Discussions on the EXIM-China loan focused on the need to ensure that it does not undermine fiscal and debt sustainability. The authorities concurred with staff on the risks of taking on such a large loan, given Grenada’s high debt level and the uncertain global outlook. Staff also pointed out the inherent conflict between the authorities desire to reduce debt, including by seeking debt relief from the Paris Club and other external creditors, while taking on the burden of such a big loan. The authorities reiterated their commitment to confirm the viability of the project by an internationally reputed third party and to limit their exposure to a minority share, as agreed under the ECF program. In addition, the authorities have committed, to update their debt sustainability analysis (DSA), in consultation with Fund staff, to examine the implications of such borrowing on debt vulnerability and ensure that this does not jeopardize debt sustainability and the objective of reducing debt in line with ECCB guidelines.

20. The Debt Sustainability Analysis (DSA) continues to show that Grenada is at risk of high external debt distress, the same as in the previous DSA.12 Based on Grenada’s average CPIA rating of “medium” the thresholds for the present value (PV) of external debt to GDP and to exports are 40 and 150 percent, respectively. In 2011, these ratios are projected at 89.3 and 304.7, respectively (a couple of percentages of GDP higher compared to the previous DSA reflecting the new loan for the agriculture feeder road project) and are projected to fall below the thresholds only in 2024 and 2020, respectively. In terms of debt service ratios, debt service to exports although currently below the 20 percent threshold will rise in 2012 and stay above the threshold for the period of analysis. However, the debt service to revenue ratio remains below the threshold of 35 percent throughout the entire period. There are important risks to the debt outlook coming from the loan from EXIM-China, which would exacerbate further the breaches of the DSA thresholds and would jeopardize the underlying objective of reaching the ECCB recommended target of 60 percent by 2020. The existing program limits do not accommodate this loan.
B.   Financial Sector Vulnerabilities
Banking Sector

21. The authorities and staff agreed on the need to closely monitor banking sector developments. The restructuring of NPLs to prevent delinquencies is a cause for concern as not only does it mask the true deterioration in the loan portfolio but also may not be sustainable going forward if the economy does not improve. In addition, liquid assets to total assets at 21 percent, although in line with prudential regulations, have declined by 2 percentage points in the last six months, as have provisioning ratios.
C.   Non-Banking Financial Sector

22. Progress on resolving the fallout from the collapse of the CL Financial Group remains slow. A ministerial sub-committee is working with interested strategic partners to set up a new company to take over BAICO’s ECCU operations. ECCU governments have agreed to inject $\mathrm{USS75}$ million in the new company, but uncertainty about the contribution of Trinidad and Tobago is complicating negotiations. In addition, the authorities together with their regional partners will limit the fiscal costs through revision of existing terms, including lowering of interest rates, extending tenors, and converting some liabilities into equity. Meanwhile, most institutional investors have started to provision, with some beginning to book the losses against their liabilities to BAICO and CLICO. However, some small credit unions and the building and loan society, whose balance sheets are weak, are said to be having difficulties in provisioning. GARFIN is attempting to enhance monitoring of these
institutions and expects that the small credit unions will merge with some of their bigger brethren.

23. The government is continuing its efforts to improve the supervision of the nonbank financial sector. The new Money Services Act passed last year and the new Insurance Act enacted earlier this year have been important in reinforcing GARFIN’s supervisory role. A new Cooperatives Societies Act has been submitted to parliament and is expected to be enacted by early-2011. On-site inspections of two small, but weak, insurance companies were completed. In parallel, offsite supervision is also being maintained, particularly of the large and systematically important companies. To further strengthen the nonbank financial sector, the authorities plan to:

 Complete on-site inspection of two additional insurance companies by end-March 2011, including one that is systemically important (structural benchmar for second review). All insurance companies will continue to be monitored through periodic off-site inspection.   
 Complete the on-site inspection of the money services company (structural benchmark for second review).   
 Conduct off-site and on-site supervision of credit unions and building societies, including closely monitoring the effects of the expected write-offs of the sector’s estimated exposure to BAICO and CLICO.   
 Continue to build the capacity of GARFIN through participation of its staff in regional training initiatives.   
 Liquidation of Capital Bank is still pending in the absence of court permission. Wh the authorities’ November 2008 petition to the High Court for the liquidation of Capital Bank has not been acted upon due to challenges by the bank’s owner, it wi proceed as soon as Court permission is granted. No associated fiscal costs are expected.

24. The authorities are continuing their efforts to comply with the internationally agreed standards aimed at reducing tax evasion in international financial centers. Thirteen Tax Information Exchange Agreements (TIEAs) have been concluded so far, putting Grenada on the OECD’s “white list”. The authorities are aiming to sign two additional TIEAs by end-2010. The authorities are also planning to undertake a study to gauge the prospects for this sector. The mission advised that given the small size of this sector, and the enhanced supervisory standards now required, further development of this sector may not be cost effective.
D.   Structural Reforms

25. The authorities agreed with the mission that the private sector remains key to sustainable growth, and further improvements in the business climate are needed to unlock its potential. Progress to date includes a reduction in the number of days it takes to start a business from 20 to 11 days. In addition, simplified procedures for property registration and the establishment of a separate land registry have been put in place during the last year. Contract enforcement is expected to be improved by the appointment of an additional judge to assist with the backlog of court cases. The authorities are also working on decreasing the number of steps needed to obtain construction permits.

26. While staff agreed on the need for diversifying growth sectors, it emphasized the need to let the private sector take the lead and the importance of lowering labor costs to improve competitiveness. There has been some investment in the agri-business sector, including establishment of a small factory to manufacture nutmeg-related products by a local investor. The government has also undertaken a study on how spillovers from St. George’s University to the rest of the economy can be improved; this is of added importance as a complement to the highly cyclical construction and tourism sectors. In this context, staff noted that the competitor-based real exchange rate has been trending up modestly and that Grenada has lost regional tourism market share in the last years. The authorities are considering participating in the building of a luxury hotel to boost Grenada’s potential as a high-end tourism destination. Staff however underscored the need to let the private sector take the lead given the government’s financing constraints and the high level of debt.

27. The authorities are working to enhance the efficiency of social assistance. A Social Safety Net Committee has been set up to spearhead reforms to better target the needs of vulnerable groups and streamline social assistance programs. A social protection policy is expected to be in place by mid-2011. By end-2011 the authorities expect to complete the following with World Bank assistance:

28. A full Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP) is being prepared. The Country Poverty Assessment in combination with the National Strategic Development Plan will form the basis of the PRSP, which is being developed with the help of CDB-funded consultants. While the full PRSP is being finalized, the authorities intend to put in place a quarterly indicator of spending on the three major cash transfer programs, which constitute the bulk of social spending. Once the PRSP is in place in 2011, this indicator will be revised to reflect priority spending areas and it will become an indicative target for the program (see MEFP, paragraph 26).
V.   PROGRAM ISSUES

29. The Attached Letter of Intent and accompanying Supplementary Memorandum of Economic Policies outline the authorities’ policy objectives for the remainder of 2010 and 2011. Quantitative performance criteria for end-December 2010 and end-June 2011 and structural measures are shown in Tables 1 and 2 in Appendix 2. Table 1 also includes a modification reflecting the authorities’ request that non-concessional borrowing limit for end-December 2010 be increased to accommodate the recently contracted loan from Kuwait and OPEC.

30. The authorities are strongly committed to the program, but implementation risks remain. If the economic recovery stalls, it could result in revenue shortfalls and difficulties in reigning in spending. While the authorities agree with staff’s views on the conditions under which the EXIM-China loan could be compatible with program requirements, there is also a risk that a lack of economic recovery and reluctance of the private sector to take on risks may result in a suboptimal solution with the authorities taking a majority stake in the venture.

31. The authorities intend to discuss further debt relief from their bilateral creditors. The authorities plan to request a stock treatment of their debt from the Paris Club (2.4 percent of total debt) and to ask non-Paris Club official bilateral creditors for similar treatment (8.6 percent of total debt). This could help to close the small financing gap projected for the second half of next year. In the absence of such debt relief, the authorities agreed to take additional measures to be discussed during the mission for the second review.
VI.   STAFF APPRAISAL

32. Grenada’s economic activity shows few signs of a recovery. High debt levels, in combination with the regional currency board arrangement, constrained possible policy responses to the crisis. In addition, the collapse of the CL Financial group heightened financial sector uncertainty and will likely entail some fiscal costs.

33. Staff welcomed the authorities’ success in meeting all the program targets, notwithstanding the difficult circumstances. All the quantitative performance criteria and all structural benchmarks for the first review were also met. With the help of quarterly budget reviews the authorities successfully contained spending to meet both the fiscal targets for the primary balance excluding grants and the reduction of domestic arrears. They also met all the structural benchmarks under the program, both on the fiscal and financial fronts.

34. That the government proceeded with the introduction of a VAT notwithstanding stiff resistance is an indication of its commitment to structural reforms. Staff underscored the importance of ensuring that the recent temporal tax exemptions are not extended and urged the authorities to boost compliance to ensure that revenues are not undermined. Staff also urged the authorities to submit the new Customs Act to parliament and move expeditiously with establishment of the ASYCUDA World and riskbased audits at customs ports.

35. Staff commended the authorities for their resolve to pursue fiscal consolidation to put debt on a declining path. The adoption of a three-year rolling budget starting from the 2011 budget will promote more stable fiscal management and provide an anchor for expectations. In this context, staff urged the authorities to improve the management and efficiency of spending, including through the passage of the Public Procurement Act. Staff also welcomed the continuation of quarterly budget reviews to ensure that annual targets are achieved to ensure the debt ratio trajectory on a downward path.

36. While recognizing the need for stimulating growth, staff expressed strong reservations about the loan from the EXIM-China. Staff appreciated the need to undertake projects that generate both employment and growth, but clearly expressed its reservations regarding such a massive project and the concentration of risks this would imply, especially given the country’s already high debt level, the risks to debt sustainability, and the significant downside risks to the global recovery. In this context, staff acknowledged the authorities’ commitment to ensure that the loan is concessional, and that the decision to embark on the project will be based on a thorough objective assessment of its economic and financial returns from an internationally reputed third party, and that the private sector will have a majority stake in the project.

37. Some modest deterioration in banking sector soundness indicators underscores the need to monitor developments carefully. While non-performing loans have remained stable since last year, it underestimates the true underlying problems with the loan portfolio given the restructuring of several of these loans. With economic activity still sluggish and unemployment persistently high, downside risks remain dominant and delinquencies could very well increase. In this context, the decline in provisioning ratios is also of concern.

38. Staff welcomed the progress in enhancing supervision of insurance companies and urged the authorities to carefully monitor financial sector vulnerabilities. GARFIN made commendable progress in the last six months and completed the on-site inspection of two small, but weak, insurance companies and plans to embark on on-site inspection of a large, systemically important insurance company. Statutory funds have also been pledged by most insurance companies and now cover more than half of the liabilities of the sector. With respect to the two failed insurance companies, BAICO and CLICO, staff welcomed the close cooperation among regional governments to address the fallout jointly. However, staff urged the authorities to limit fiscal costs by revising existing terms of the deposit-like instruments, including through lowering of interest rates, extending tenors, and converting of some liabilities into equity. The government should proceed directly to liquidation of Capital Bank as soon as the court permits.

39. Staff welcomed the authorities’ efforts to encourage private sector-led growth, including by seeking strategic investors. The establishment of a separate land registry and appointment of a new judge to expedite the backlog of existing court cases are welcome steps. More recently, authorities’ efforts to seek strategic investors, including both domestic and international ones, are steps in the right direction.

40. Staff welcomed the government’s decision to improve the social safety net. Streamlining and better targeting will help to ensure that vulnerable groups are covered. Staff also encouraged the completion of the full Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper as early as possible.

41. The program is subject to a number of risks. Financing of the external current account deficit, especially projected donor support and private inflows, is subject to some uncertainty. Despite the authorities’ commitments, there are downside risks to the fiscal consolidation path. If the recovery stalls, the planned revenue improvements may not materialize and there could be increased pressures for further tax exemptions and spending. In addition, the fiscal costs related to the CL Financial Group and the large external borrowing under consideration, if it were to happen, could very well undermine the achievement of the debt objectives under the program. The limited implementation capacity could delay the completion of some reforms or give rise to slippages in data reporting.

42. In view of the authorities’ meeting all targets under the current ECF arrangement, and their strong commitment to the program, staff supports the authorities’ request for completion of the first review, the modification of the performance criterion and the financing assurances review, notwithstanding the above mentioned risks.
Proposed Decision
The following decision, which may be adopted by a majority of the votes cast, is proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:
First Review Under the ECF Arrangement, Modification of Performance Criteria, and Financing Assurances Review

1. Grenada has consulted with the Fund in accordance with paragraph 4.A(b) of the arrangement for Grenada under the Extended Credit Facility (ECF) (EBS/10/57, 3/25/10) (the “ECF Arrangement”) to review program implementation and financing assurances, and to reach understandings regarding the conditions for further disbursements during the second year of the ECF Arrangement.

2. The letter dated November 2, 2010 from the Minister for Finance (the “November 2010 Letter”) together with its Supplementary Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies (the “November 2010 Memorandum”) and Technical Memorandum of Understanding (the “November 2010 TMU”) shall be attached to the ECF Arrangement for Grenada, and the letter dated March 23, 2010, from the Minister for Finance shall be read as supplemented and modified by the November 2010 Letter and its attachments.
3. Accordingly, the ECF Arrangement for Grenada shall be amended as follows:
a. A new paragraph 2(d) shall be included to read as follows:
“2(d) the fourth disbursement, in an amount equivalent to SDR 1.250 million, will be available on or after November 15, 2011, at the request of Grenada and subject to paragraphs 4 and 5 below;”

b. Paragraph 4.A(a) shall be amended as follows: The language “as set out in Table 1 of the MEFP and further specified in the TMU” shall be replaced with “as set out in Table 1 of the November 2010 Memorandum and further specified in the November 2010 TMU”.
c. A new paragraph 4.B shall be included to read as follows: “The fourth disbursement under this arrangement specified in paragraph 2(d) above:
(a) if the Managing Director of the Trustee finds that with respect to the fourth disbursement, the data as of June 30, 2011, indicate that:

(i) the floor on the central government primary balance excluding grants, or (ii) the ceiling on the stock of central government domestic arrears, or (iii) the ceiling on the contracting or guaranteeing of non-concessional external debt by the central government with maturity of at least one year, or (iv) the ceiling on the contracting or guaranteeing of bilateral concessional external debt by the central government with maturity of at least one year,
as set out in Table 1 of the November 2010 Memorandum and further specified in the November 2010 TMU was not observed; or
(b) until the Trustee has determined that, with respect to the fourth disbursement the third program review, referred to in paragraph 28 of the November 2010 Memorandum, has been completed.”

d. Paragraphs 5(a) and 5(b) shall be amended as follows: In each paragraph, the words “as set out in Table 1 of the MEFP and further specified in the TMU” shall be deleted and replaced with “as specified in Table 1 of the November 2010 Memorandum, and further specified in the November 2010 TMU”.

4. The Fund decides that the first review specified in paragraph 4.A(b), and the financing assurances review specified in paragraph 5(d), of the ECF Arrangement for Grenada are completed, and that Grenada may request the second disbursement.

text: Annex: Summary of Appendices
The full appendices to this report are issued as a supplement and can be viewed in CyberDocs on the Fund’s intranet and on the secure extranet for Executive Directors and member officials.
Fund Relations

Grenada’s outstanding purchases as of end-September 2010 amounted to SDR 17.66 million (150.90 percent of quota). Grenada is a member of the ECCB, which manages monetary policy and the exchange system for its eight members. The common currency, the Eastern Caribbean dollar, has been pegged at $\operatorname{ECS}2.70$ per U.S. dollar since July 1976. The last Article IV Consultation was concluded by the Executive Board on November 4, 2009 (EBS/09/168). CARTAC, MCM, FAD, and LEG have provided extensive technical assistance. A safeguards assessment was completed in 2007, and concluded that the ECCB continues to have appropriate control mechanisms in place. Grenada accepted the obligations of Article VIII, Sections 2, 3, and 4 in January 1994. It maintains an exchange system free of restrictions on the making of payments and transfers for current international transactions.
Relations with the World Bank Group1

In June 2010 the World Bank endorsed the Organization of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS) Regional Partnership Strategy for FY 10–14. The strategy seeks to support economic stability, competitiveness, and inclusive and sustained growth. The FY 10–14 Strategy will support the sub-region’s development agenda through two main pillars: (1) building resilience, by promoting fiscal and debt sustainability, improving social safety nets, education and health, and developing climate resilience; and (2) enhancing competitiveness and stimulating sustainable growth, by improving the countries’ domestic financial sectors through enhanced regulatory environment and supervisory frameworks for financial institutions. There are eight active World Bank projects in Grenada for a net commitment of approximately $\mathrm{USS}32.6$ million of which US\$24 million has been disbursed.
Relations with the Caribbean Development Bank2
During 2009, the Caribbean Development Bank (CDB) approved the second Country Strategy Paper (CSP) for Grenada, which outlines the Bank’s strategy in Grenada over

2009-11. The resource envelope of US\$26 million blends the Bank’s resources to yield a 35 percent grant element in conformity with Grenada’s debt sustainability borrowing terms. The strategy supports macroeconomic management, access to quality education, access by the poor to basic infrastructure, the social policy framework, agricultural output, road access for rural development, the land use policy framework, and reduction of climate change related risks.
Statistical Issues

Grenada participates in the Fund’s General Data Dissemination System (GDDS). Although data provision is sufficient for program monitoring, significant improvement is needed to facilitate effective surveillance by addressing weaknesses in coverage, timeliness and frequency data. Planned mid-October 2010 revisions of real GDP and CPI series are expected to result in significant improvement of data quality and reliability.
Attachment I. Letter of Intent
St. George’s, Grenada November 02, 2010
Mr. Dominique Strauss-Kahn Managing Director International Monetary Fund Washington, D.C. 20431
Dear Mr. Strauss-Kahn:

The attached Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies (MEFP) discusses progress in implementing our current ECF-supported program. With the global recovery still very fragile, we continue to face a very challenging period ahead. The two most important sectors of our economy—tourism and construction, the latter driven to a large extent by FDI— continue to be hard hit by the lack of a sustained recovery in the U.S. and Europe. FDI flows are expected to be lower this year than last year and all FDI-related projects remain at a virtual standstill. As a result, activity is expected to be flat this year and unemployment to remain high. Given our high debt levels and limited financing options, support from our development partners, including the IMF, the Caribbean Development Bank, the World Bank and the European Union, has been important in providing resources to help dampen the negative impact of the recession on our economy.

Despite the difficult economic environment, we remain firmly committed to sound economic and fiscal management. Toward this end, we met all program-related quantitative performance criteria for end June. We have also met all the structural benchmarks, both on the fiscal and financial sectors. In view of this, we request completion of the first review, modification of the performance criterion on non-concessional debt and the release of the second disbursement (SDR 1.25 million) under the ECF.

As in many other countries, we are facing a difficult challenge: generating sustained growth while reducing debt. With the private sector holding back on investment and employment, we believe the government can play a catalytic role to stimulate growth.

The issue of restoring growth is the biggest macro-economic challenge facing our country at this time. I fully concur with your insightful remarks at the opening session of the recently concluded Annual Meetings when you said and I quote “the biggest threat to fiscal sustainability is low growth”.

The recent Regional Economic Outlook from the Fund shows that tourism has been a significant contributor to growth in the region. It should be noted that Tourism is also one of the five sectors that we have identified in our own growth strategy for the country, especially given the relatively small size of this sector and the considerable scope for its expansion.

Toward this end, we have submitted an application for a concessional loan of US\$115 million from the Export-Import Bank of China. We intend to use this loan to catalyze private sector involvement in the stalled project to build a luxury hotel.
We believe that this project could generate significant socio-economic benefits for Grenada in the medium and long term.  These include:
The project will also boost the construction sector in the short term.

Consistent with our commitment under the ECF programme, we will limit our exposure to a minority share and seek an objective assessment of the economic and financial returns of this project from an internationally reputed third party.
We are mindful of the risks and will proceed only if prospective benefits from this project clearly outweigh the associated additional debt servicing burden from the expected concessional financing.

We are preparing a full Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP) to be known as the Growth and Poverty Reduction Strategy (GPRS) and we are stepping up our ability to monitor social spending. The Country Poverty Assessment in combination with the National Strategic Development Plan will form the basis of the PRSP, which is being developed with the help of CDB-funded consultants.  We intend to integrate the costed programs starting with the 2012 budget.

We believe that the proposed policies described in the accompanying MEFP are sufficient to achieve our program objectives. Nevertheless, we are committed to any further measures that may become necessary for achieving the program’s objectives.

We will consult with the Fund on the adoption of these measures and revisions to the policies contained in the MEFP, in accordance with the Fund’s policies on such issues. In this context, once the expected contribution of Grenada to the resolution of BAICO becomes clear, we will consult with Fund staff. We will also continue to provide the Fund any information it may request to monitor and assess the implementation of the economic and financial policies.
As in the past, we intend to make public the contents of this letter, the attached MEFP and Technical Memorandum of Understanding, as well as the accompanying staff report.
Yours faithfully,
Attachment II. Supplementary Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies

1. We continue to be guided by our Letter of Intent and Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies dated March 23, 2010 (MEFP). This supplement lays out our economic objectives for the forthcoming period. The fundamental objectives of our economic strategy remain mitigating the stresses created by the global crisis and the failures in the regional financial sector while ensuring that public finances are sustainable, improving the climate for private sector investment, promoting sustained high economic growth and alleviating poverty.
I.   RECENT ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENTS

2. The recovery from last year’s significant output contraction is yet to take place, with flat growth expected for 2010. The two main sectors of the economy—tourism and construction—continue to be hard hit by the lack of a sustained recovery in the U.S. and Europe. Tourist arrivals continue to decline, falling by 9.4 percent during the first half of this year compared with the same period in 2009 while construction activity declined by 15.9 percent during the same period. FDI flows to Grenada are expected to be even lower than in 2009. However, we have seen some promising developments in Grenada’s agriculture sector notwithstanding the drought in the first half of the year. But the sector at present is too small to alone generate a turnaround in employment and growth. Furthermore, there are significant downside risks given the still uncertain outlook in developed countries, the major source of tourism and FDI for Grenada. Inflation increased to 6.3 percent in September, reflecting both the one-off price effects from the introduction of VAT on February 1, 2010, but also the pass-through from higher international fuel prices compared to last year. However, once the initial effect of the VAT tapers off, average 2010 inflation is projected to decline to 5 percent and to 2.2 percent next year.

3. Despite the weak real economy, the fiscal outturn through August was in line with program projections. Revenue collection for the first half of 2010 was in line with expectations. Although spending on goods and services was somewhat higher than expected (in part due to higher-than-assumed fuel prices), we managed to contain overall spending, especially by limiting capital outlays. While we remain committed to meet our annual budget target, we have given some tax exemptions to stimulate the economy and generate much-needed employment. In particular, we have provided some temporary and strictly timebound tax relief to critical sectors that are the mainstay of the economy, notably tourism and construction.

4. Private sector credit remains sluggish, while the non-performing loans (NPLs) might not yet have bottomed out. Private sector credit grew by 4.4 percent (y/y) through August, compared with 4.5 percent at end-2009, as most banks have tightened lending standards given the uncertainty in the economic outlook. Broad money growth also remains very low at 0.3 percent (y/y) at end-August. The ratio of NPLs to total loans remained at
5.9 percent at end-June, the same as at end-December 2009, although this includes restructuring of loans to keep delinquency rates down, a process that will likely continue through 2011.
II.   PROGRAM PERFORMANCE
5. All program-related quantitative performance criteria for end June were met.

The introduction of the VAT in February 2010 has been very successful and revenue collections have been in line with expectations. We received higher than expected grants, and as agreed with the IMF, this allowed us to accommodate higher spending while still remaining within the adjusted budget deficit target under the program for end-June. We have met all the program targets:
6. We also met the structural benchmarks under the program In line with our commitment:

 We have conducted two quarterly budget reviews, at end-April and end-July, followed by the issuance of finance circulars to keep expenditures under control.   
 The VAT unit has been integrated as a new division in the Inland Revenue Department, and officers in the new division are carrying out VAT audits.   
C Under the leadership of the newly-appointed Accountant General, we have made an inventory of all the various government bank accounts and have made significant progress in streamlining them in an efficient fashion. By April of next year we expect to have closed all unnecessary bank accounts and established a Single Treasury Account System.   
 On the financial sector side, the Grenada Authority for the Regulation of Financial Institutions (GARFIN) has worked closely with the insurance companies to set up statutory funds, and more than half of the total liabilities of the insurance companies have been pledged to GARFIN. We are making efforts to place these assets under trusteeship, but this process has taken longer than expected due to the need to sensitize banks on the trustee requirements of the new Insurance Act. We expect to have this process completed by end-January 2011.

Using financial vulnerabilities as selection criteria, we have completed the onsite supervision of two small weak insurance companies. By early next year, we plan to have also completed the inspection of a large, systemically important insurance company.
III.   OBJECTIVES AND POLICIES FOR THE REST OF 2010 AND 2011

7. Notwithstanding the difficult global and domestic environment, we remain firmly committed to sound economic and fiscal management. Our key objectives are: continue to pursue fiscal consolidation in order to reduce the debt service burden on our revenues which will free up resources for other priority spending and place the economy on a sustainable debt trajectory; resolve the strains in the financial sector caused by the collapse of the CL Financial Group and strengthen the financial sector to restore the public’s confidence in it; and improve the business and investment climate to achieve broad-based economic growth, generate employment  and reduce poverty.
A.   Ensuring fiscal and debt sustainability

8. Given our high debt level (122 percent of GDP at end-2009) we have focused our attention on bringing it down to sustainable levels. We were able to contain spending in the first half of the year as planned, by working closely with other ministries to cut back nonpriority spending. At the same time, we redoubled our efforts to seek grant financing from donors. Recognizing our efforts to minimize the accumulation of additional debt while also trying to maintain spending in critical priority areas, several donors have stepped in to provide grants. The European Union has pledged $\mathrm{ECS~}11.7$ million in the context of the Vulnerability Flex facility (expected by December 2010). In addition Morocco and Venezuela have also committed to provide grants. These disbursements together with our efforts to expedite all public sector projects will help to generate both employment and growth without increasing our debt.
9. The temporary tax relief measures to some hard-hit sectors were deemed necessary given the continued difficult economic environment. These measure include

A 50 percent reduction in the VAT rate on hotel accommodation from September to November, 2010 to limit job losses during the off-peak season (with a fiscal cost of 0.06 percent of GDP in 2010).   
C A 50 percent reduction in the VAT rate on building materials (steel, cement, roofing materials, and lumber) between October 1, 2010—March 31, 2011 (estimated cost 0.05 percent of GDP).   
 A one-year removal of the excise tax on the importation of commercial vehicles (estimated cost 0.1 percent of GDP).

10. In order to offset the loss in revenue from these measures and ensure the integrity of the newly introduced VAT system, we will (i) keep these tax measures temporary as currently announced, (ii) enforce compliance, including through taxpayer education and service programs to facilitate voluntary compliance with filing and payment requirements, and (iii) expedite the ongoing establishment of a single unique Tax Identification Number.

11. We are making two permanent changes to the VAT system in order to harmonize it with neighboring countries and to meet our medium and long-term economic growth objectives. First, the VAT on the service charge for hotels has been eliminated on condition that no less than 90 percent of this service charge is paid to workers as in some other countries in the region using the VAT (estimated foregone revenue from this measure is 0.03 percent of 2010 GDP). Second, all tertiary level textbooks will be exempted from VAT to ensure that our young generation has access to affordable education and improves its human capital to match the growing need for skilled workers (estimated revenue loss from this measure is 0.1 percent of 2010 GDP). Parliament is expected to pass the amended VAT Law, including these two permanent changes as well as the expiration date of the above-mentioned temporary concessions, by end 2010 (proposed structural benchmark for second review).

12. We are continuing to make progress in customs reforms. After some delay due to the need to bring it in line with international good practice and improve the efficiency of customs collections, the new Customs Act will be submitted to Parliament by November, and it is expected that it will be passed by Parliament by end-year. The pilot project for updating the customs technology platform (ASYCUDA World) is well under way, and we plan to start implementing the new system by end-March 2011 (proposed structural benchmark for second review). Following the successful implementation of this new platform, we expect to establish risk-based inspections, thus increasing the efficiency of customs revenue collection.

13. We are also undertaking several reforms to improve expenditure efficiency and management. While there have been some delays in the passage of the Public Procurement Act, procurement reform remains a high priority. We plan to ensure that the Public Procurement Act is passed by end-2010 and the Public Procurement Unit becomes fully functional by end-March 2011 (proposed structural benchmark for the second review). To generate further savings, the Waste Reduction Unit, which is soon to be fully staffed, will be tasked with outlining an action plan to rationalize expenditure in telecommunications, streamline the use and maintenance of vehicles and limit electricity charges by conserving energy. In addition, earlier this year we completed the Public Expenditure and Financial Accountability (PEFA) performance measurement framework. Based on the PEFA and with the assistance of CARTAC, we have finalized an Action Plan that includes several measures, including the establishment of a Single Treasury System (proposed structural benchmark for second review), and instituting a comprehensive commitment control system (proposed structural benchmark for third review). We are also working with CARTAC to adopt the new

Chart of Accounts which will improve the transparency of fiscal operations and help us track and identify further areas for saving. We are also committed to reducing the public sector wage bill since it is already one of the highest among in the ECCU and leaves little room to other worthwhile expenditures. Negotiations with the unions are ongoing, and we are proposing no salary increases for 2009 and 2010.

14. The Debt Management Unit is developing its work program which is aimed at improving debt management and reducing the cost of borrowing. We have hired an experienced professional to help in this endeavor and staff is undergoing extensive training in DSA preparation and the compilation of a quarterly debt bulletin, eventually to be presented to Cabinet (proposed structural benchmark for third review). The unit expects to complete a first draft of a debt management strategy by end-October 2010, and once the finalized strategy is complete, a borrowing plan is expected to be put in place by end-December 2010 (proposed structural benchmark for the second review).

15. We plan to further reduce the stock of domestic arrears. Over the last several months we have successfully reduced the overall stock of unpaid claims from $\mathrm{ECS}60.8$ million (3.5 percent of GDP) at the beginning of the ECF arrangement to $\operatorname{ECS}$ 44 million (2.5 percent of GDP) at end-June 2010. By the end of this year we plan to reduce these arrears to $\operatorname{ECS}26.8$ million (1.5 percent of GDP). We have also significantly brought down the amount of domestic arrears older than 60 days, from $\mathrm{ECS}22.7$ million (1.3 percent of GDP) to $\mathrm{ECS~}14.3$ million (0.8 percent of GDP) during the same period, and although they have crept up recently we are committed to reducing them further to $\mathrm{ECS}12.5$ million (0.8 percent of GDP) by end-2010.

16.      In light of the severe financing constraints we are facing, we have received increased budgetary support from our multilateral donors. In addition to the grants mentioned above, multilateral financial institutions have provided resources to help us tide over difficult times. The World Bank provided $\mathrm{US}\mathbb{S}$ 8 million under the Development Policy Loan (DPL) and the Caribbean Development Bank provided $\mathrm{US}\$6.4$ million in a first tranche under the Policy Based Loan (PBL) with a second similarly-sized disbursement of $\mathrm{US}\S6.4$ million expected by end-2010. The European Union has also provided $\epsilon2.8$ million in budgetary support. This support, together with the support under the previous and current ECF-supported programs from the International Monetary Fund have provided much-needed resources for the budget and helped to ease the fiscal adjustment during the crisis. The disbursement of additional funds from the IMF on completion of this review will also help us meet some of our budget obligations.

17. We have applied for a concessional loan from the Export-Import Bank of China to build a luxury hotel in partnership with private investors, though we are fully cognizant of the downside risks. While this project could generate substantial economic benefits by creating employment and boosting Grenada’s tourism potential over the medium and long term, we are fully aware of the downside risks given the uncertainty of a speedy

global recovery and the associated sluggish recovery in tourism demand this may entail. We will, therefore, consult with an internationally reputed third party for an objective assessment of the economic and financial returns of such a project before we agree to a public-private partnership. We will also ensure that the majority ownership rests with the private sector. In consultation with Fund staff, we will update our debt sustainability analysis (DSA) to examine the implications of such borrowing on our debt vulnerability and ensure that we keep our commitment to reducing debt in line with ECCB guidelines.

18. We intend to approach the Paris Club to request additional debt relief (2.4 percent of total debt). Debt service (in percent of revenues) has doubled since 2006, when we received a flow rescheduling of our debt from the Paris Club following the devastating hurricanes. That rescheduling allowed us to negotiate comparable terms with most of our creditors and provided much relief. We are continuing our best efforts to reach agreement with the remaining creditors—Russian Federation and Taiwan, Province of China—to which we have put forward a rescheduling proposal. The Great Recession has hit us hard, growth has declined and unemployment is rising. Despite these unprecedented economic challenges, we have established a track record of successful fiscal consolidation and engagement with the IMF under the previous and current ECF arrangements. A reduction in debt service will provide us some fiscal space to counteract the negative impact of this shock. Therefore, we intend to approach the Paris Club for a rescheduling of our debt and then seek comparable treatment from all other external creditors.
B.   Reducing vulnerabilities in the financial system

19. The collapse of the Trinidad and Tobago-based CL Financial Group represents a major challenge to financial system stability. BAICO is estimated to have a negative net worth of some $\operatorname{ECS}0.8$ billion (6 percent of ECCU GDP) in the ECCU. There are no estimates available for CLICO at this point. Last year judicial managers for the British American Insurance Company (BAICO) reported that the company was insolvent and recommended the creation of a new company to take over its ECCU operations. A ministerial sub-committee is working with interested strategic partners to set up a new company. While uncertainty remains about the contribution of Trinidad and Tobago, we are continuing to make efforts to resolve this issue as expeditiously as possible.

20. We will continue our best efforts, through the regional solution, to set limits on fiscal costs. While it is almost certain that ECCU governments will need to inject capital, we together with our regional partners, will try to limit the fiscal costs through revision of existing terms, including lowering of interest rates, extending tenors, and converting some liabilities into equity.

21. The government is continuing its effort to improve the supervision of the nonbank financial sector. Last year we passed a new Money Services Act and a new Insurance Act to enhance GARFIN’s supervisory powers. A new Cooperatives Society Act has been submitted to parliament and is expected to be enacted by early 2011. To further strengthen the nonbank financial sector, we intend to:

Complete on-site inspection of two additional insurance companies by end-March 2011, including one that is systemically important (structural benchmark for second review). We are also continuing to closely monitor all insurance companies through periodic off-site inspection. Complete the on-site inspection of the two money services companies operating in Grenada (structural benchmark for second review). Conduct off-site and on-site supervision of credit unions and building societies, including closely monitoring the effects of the expected write-offs of the sector’s estimated exposure to CLICO: Strengthening the framework for Anti-Money Laundering and Combating the Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT).  Continue to build the capacity of GARFIN through participation of our staff in regional training initiatives.

22. We will proceed directly to liquidation of Capital Bank as soon as the court permits. We petitioned the High Court for the liquidation of Capital Bank in November 2008, but the liquidation has been delayed because of challenges by the bank’s owner. The liquidation will proceed as soon as Court permission is granted. We do not expect to take on any associated fiscal costs.

23. We are continuing our efforts to comply with an internationally agreed standard aimed at reducing tax evasion in international financial centers. Even though the international financial sector in Grenada is very small, we have concluded 13 Tax Information Exchange Agreements (TIEAs) so far, which is above the 12 required for removal from the OECD “gray list”, and are aiming to sign two additional TIEAs by end-2010.
C.   Generating high and sustainable growth and reducing poverty

24. The private sector remains key to sustainable growth, but to enhance its economic contribution much remains to be done. Progress to date includes a reduction in the number of days it takes to start a business from 20 to 11 days. In addition, simplified procedures for property registration and the establishment of a separate land registry have been put in place during the last year. Contract enforcement is expected to be improved by the appointment of an additional judge to assist with the backlog of court cases.

25. We are instituting several reforms to our social safety net program to ensure that we effectively target the most vulnerable. We have set up a social safety net committee to spearhead reform efforts and elaborate an action plan for reform based on the 2009 UN/WB report on Social Safety Net. We aim to have a social protection policy in place by mid-2011. By end-2011 we also expect to complete the following with the assistance of the World Bank:

26. We are preparing a full Poverty Reduction Strategy Paper (PRSP). The Country Poverty Assessment in combination with the National Strategic Development Plan will form the basis of the PRSP, which is being developed with the help of CDB-funded consultants. We hope to have a draft PRSP by end-2010 and start the stakeholder consultation process soon thereafter. We intend to integrate the costed programs starting with the 2012 budget.

27. Given our strong commitment to poverty reduction in these challenging economic times, we are stepping up our ability to monitor social spending. We will put in place a quarterly indicator of social spending, to ensure that we do not shortchange vulnerable groups. The indicator will encompass spending on the three major cash transfer programs, which constitute the bulk of social spending. Once the PRSP is in place in 2011, we will revise this indicator to reflect priority spending areas and make it an indicative target for the program.
IV.   OTHER ISSUES
A.   Program Monitoring

28.      We will continue to closely monitor economic developments and program performance. Two Ministry of Finance officials have been designated to coordinate and monitor the macroeconomic and structural program targets. We will continue to conduct monthly monitoring of domestic arrears and below-the-line financing of the central government’s overall balance. We will also work closely with the ECCB and commercial banks to reconcile data on bank lending to the government on a monthly basis. The performance criteria for end-December 2010 and end-June 2011 and indicative target for end-March 2011 are specified in Table 1 and further specified in the Technical Memorandum of Understanding. Structural benchmarks for the second and third reviews are specified in Table 2. We anticipate that the second review would take place in May 2011 and the third review in November 2011.
B.   Reducing Natural Disaster Vulnerabilities

29. We will continue to actively participate in regional initiatives to pool climate risks. Since 2007, we have participated in the World Bank’s Caribbean Catastrophic Risk Insurance Facility (CCRIF), the world’s first ever multi-country catastrophe insurance pool.
C.   Fiscal transparency

30. We are continuing our efforts to improve fiscal transparency. We began disseminating to the public quarterly information (with a one-quarter lag) on the overall fiscal situation and gross financing needs of the central government from end-2009. We intend to publish financial information for the statutory bodies starting end-2010. In line with the new Public Finance Management Act of 2007, public enterprises are expected to submit audited financial statements within four months from the end of the financial year. They are also required to submit annual business plans within four months of the start of the next financial year. While compliance has been improving, and audited results for some SOEs are being presented to parliament, we plan to formalize regular reporting by all SOEs, especially on some critical indicators, so that at a minimum we are alerted about the stemming risks, both to and from this sector. We will continue to publish newly granted or extended tax concessions on a quarterly basis.
